subject: Make CategoryParameter.getOptions() unmodifiable
commit_message:
Make CategoryParameter.getOptions() unmodifiable

Change-Id: If7331577febe058d4415aab0d2336e60213fd7bb
Issue-ID: VID-344
Signed-off-by: Joanna Jeremicz <joanna.jeremicz@nokia.com>

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/vid-master-verify-java/480/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/vid-master-verify-java/480

Patch Set 1:

Hi Joanna, can you explain the motivation for this change? Thanks

Patch Set 1:

Hi Ofir,
There is already a dedicated method for adding options. Someone can change it's behavior in the future, so we would have two different ways of modifying the list and it would cause confusion.
Also, it is much safer if an object exposes only some desired operations that an user can perform on its private member. If we use a getter for this purpose, we can do anything with the set (for example erase all its elements), so it can be dangerous and difficult to debug, as it's almost the same as making the private fild public.

Patch Set 1:

At first glance change was fine, but if `CategoryParameter` supports `setOptions` (and it should support, as being a DAO) -- what the unmodifiability adds here?
Are we trying to prevent here accidental changes only?

Patch Set 1:

Hi Ittay,

As I mentioned in my previous comment, there are two main reasons behind this change:

- The setter is there for a reason. Of course we can do anything through a mutable getter but is it a good practice? Before the change we could do (and did) this:
categoryParameter.getOptions().add(categoryParameterOption);
instead of this:
categoryParameter.addOption(categoryParameterOption)
This way we had two different options to do the same thing. What if the implementation of addOption changes at some point?

- Getting the reference to the whole object means that the user of this function can do anything with the field that should be private, which is especially dangerous in a multithreded environment.

In my opinion, if there is no good reason to keep this particular getter return a mutable object, we should make it immutable because it's just safer, but if still the situation is unclear, maybe there is something I'm not aware of.

Thanks for elaborating the case.
You bought me, especially with "the setter is there for a reason". :-)
Change has been successfully merged by Ittay Stern
